Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time in the evenings to listen to lecture recordings since I learn best by hearing things, but with my extracurriculars and everything else going on, it rarely worked out consistently. I'd often end up cramming multiple topics into one long session when I realized assignments were due. I kept telling myself I'd get more organized, but the anxiety just made it harder to stick to any plan.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was coming up. Some weeks I barely touched the course materials because I was overwhelmed with other commitments, and then I'd have these intense periods right before deadlines where I'd study for hours. I wanted to be more consistent, but when you're already stressed and anxious, it's hard to maintain that discipline. Deadlines basically dictated everything for me.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or realizing I had an assignment due soon that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd see classmates talking about course content and feel that jolt of panic that I was falling behind. The discussion posts also pulled me in because I actually enjoyed connecting with other students. But honestly, fear of missing something important was probably my biggest motivator to check the LMS.
Q4: For a few weeks in the middle of the semester, I actually got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every other day. I think it happened because I started associating it with the discussion forums, which I genuinely liked participating in. But then things got really stressful with my other activities and some personal stuff, and that routine just fell apart. It's frustrating because I know consistency would have helped me so much.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them while doing other things or replay confusing parts multiple times. The discussion forums were also really valuable because hearing other students' perspectives helped concepts click in ways they didn't when I was just studying alone. I posted quite a bit there and it made me feel less isolated in my struggles.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and PDFs because honestly, reading dense text just doesn't stick for me the way listening does. I'd download them with good intentions but then never actually get through them. Some of the supplementary resources also felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to keep up with the core content. When you're anxious about falling behind, adding more material feels counterproductive.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams, or if it helped me understand something I was genuinely confused about. The discussion posts felt worthwhile because of the social connection and because explaining things to others helped me learn. If something felt like busywork or I couldn't see the point, my motivation to engage with it just tanked, especially when I was already stressed.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. When you're barely keeping up with required work and dealing with high anxiety, anything optional gets pushed to the bottom of the list. Required activities had that urgency attached to them that forced me to engage even when I didn't feel like it. I actually think I might have benefited from some optional materials, but I just couldn't justify the time.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture audio and listen to it on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also recorded myself summarizing concepts and played those back, which is a study method that really works for me. I had a study group that met in person sometimes too, where we'd talk through difficult concepts together.
Q10: There were so many times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't show it at all. Like when I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times over several days, or when I'd spend hours discussing concepts with my peer study group. I also did a lot of mental rehearsal and self-testing that obviously doesn't show up anywhere. My actual engagement with the material was way higher than my login frequency suggests.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in once to download the lecture videos or audio, and then I'd study from those files for days without touching the LMS again. It was just more convenient and less anxiety-inducing than constantly logging back in and seeing all the things I hadn't done yet. The offline studying was where most of my real learning happened.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just crashed for me. My stress levels were through the roof, my anxiety made it hard to concentrate, and I started avoiding the course because even thinking about it made me feel overwhelmed. I was also dealing with some personal stuff that ate into my energy and time. The CBT sessions I started helped eventually, but there was definitely a period where I just couldn't engage properly.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or had a lot going on with my extracurriculars, the LMS became something I'd dread opening. The cognitive load of just figuring out what to do next felt enormous when I was already depleted. Sometimes I'd log in, see everything I was behind on, and just log right back out because the anxiety was too much. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it probably was.
Q14: There were times when I'd watch videos but my mind was completely elsewhere because I was so anxious or tired. I'd get to the end and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Being physically present in the LMS didn't mean I was mentally present at all.
Q15: I definitely postponed exam preparation way more than I should have. I think it was a combination of anxiety making it feel overwhelming and some avoidance because I was scared of confirming that I didn't understand the material well enough. The irony is that avoiding it just made the anxiety worse. I knew the exams were important, but knowing something and acting on it are different things when you're struggling.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that reliably got me to engage. I'd have these bursts of activity right before things were due, which is why my assignment completion is pretty high even though my attendance and engagement were inconsistent. It's not a healthy pattern, but the pressure of a deadline cut through the anxiety paralysis in a way nothing else did.
Q17: When I got feedback, it actually motivated me to engage more, especially if it was encouraging or showed me specifically what to improve. But there were also times when critical feedback made my anxiety spike and I'd avoid that part of the course for a while. The timing mattered too—feedback that came quickly felt more useful than feedback weeks later when I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The course structure was honestly a bit overwhelming for me. Having five online courses at once meant there was always something demanding attention, and this course sometimes got deprioritized. The weekly modules were helpful in theory, but when you fall behind, they start to feel like a wall you can't climb. I needed more flexibility or check-in points to help me stay on track.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier because they gave me a reason to connect with other people, which helped with the isolation of online learning. But the sheer volume of content and the way everything was organized made it harder—I often felt lost about what to prioritize. Clearer guidance on what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me a lot given my limited bandwidth.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is really anxious or uncertain about what they're doing. They might log in repeatedly, watch videos, read through things, but feel too unsure or overwhelmed to actually submit work. It could also be perfectionism—wanting to understand everything completely before attempting an assignment. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity, especially when stress is involved.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat, though my exam scores didn't reflect it. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in my LMS activity. Someone could absolutely be studying effectively from downloaded materials, textbooks, or study groups and barely touch the LMS. The platform only captures one slice of how students actually learn, and for auditory learners like me, that slice might be pretty small.
Q22: A course log can show patterns like when you're most active, how you respond to deadlines, and whether you're participating in discussions. But it completely misses the quality of engagement—whether you're actually absorbing anything or just going through motions. It misses offline study, emotional state, and all the invisible mental work of processing and understanding concepts. It's like measuring only the tip of an iceberg.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when you want to discuss ideas with others. For me, it also means feeling connected to the learning community and believing that what I'm learning matters. True engagement is as much emotional and cognitive as it is behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting to stay engaged despite my anxiety and stress. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures on my phone or the peer study sessions that kept me going. It also wouldn't show how the CBT sessions helped me develop better coping strategies that improved my mood, even if my grades didn't reflect that yet. My journey this semester was about more than just academic performance—it was about resilience.
